summary: The post argues that people giving feedback should be mindful of the ratio of critique to affirmation they deliver, because recipients often don't know how valued they are and only remember the criticisms. Charity offers specific techniques for making positive feedback land—being specific, using the SBI framework for praise, grounding critique in overall reaction, and giving feedback that helps people grow.
key_points: Most people you value don't realize how much you appreciate them, but are acutely aware of how they fall short
Specific praise lands and sticks far better than generic compliments
Grounding critique in your overall positive reaction prevents people from hearing only negativity
Even poorly-delivered critiques can be absorbed in the context of a good relationship
Engineers tend to show interest by picking ideas apart, which can feel like pure criticism without explicit affirmation
money_quotes: I can pretty much guarantee that none of the people you love working with realize just how much you value them, but they are acutely aware of all the ways they fall short or fail you.

Even poorly-delivered critiques can be dealt with in the context of a good relationship, when the person knows how much you value them, and even the most delicately delivered criticism can be hard to hear from someone when all you ever seem to hear from them is how much you suck.
Engineers can be the worst at this, because we tend to show our interest by eagerly engaging with an idea or piece of work … by picking it apart, and chattering about all the ways it could be better.
And there's no reason to save all the nice words and praise and gratitude for someone's funeral (or when they leave the company).
key_insight: Before delivering critical feedback, consider whether the ratio of critique to affirmation you've given someone accurately represents how much you actually value them—because they almost certainly don't know.
